Bloom Energy + 100 Public Semiconductor Stocks: AI Power, Relationship Map, and Alpha Ranking

This dashboard maps Bloom Energy’s verified AI data-center power contracts and a 50 U.S. + 50 non-U.S. public semiconductor universe. “Alpha score” is a research-prioritization metric for potentially under-discovered, undervalued, brewing exposure to AI infrastructure cash flows. It is not a price target and not investment advice.

100
public semiconductor stocks mapped
2,207
relationship/cash-flow edges generated
20+
connections per company, plus depth-5 path
No gas turbines
Bloom evidence is solid-oxide fuel-cell onsite power

Method and reading rules

Important: Public filings usually disclose supply-chain dependencies, customer concentration, segment revenue, or purchase obligations, but not every named customer invoice. Where a specific order is public, the dashboard marks it as a contract/order. Where the link is an ecosystem dependency, it is marked as industry-map evidence and the cash amount is listed as undisclosed.

The alpha model weights five signals: AI exposure, value-chain centrality, discovery/crowding, valuation versus likely AI cash-flow leverage, and catalysts. It subtracts risk for customer concentration, export controls, cycle risk, negative earnings, and already-crowded ETF ownership. The megacaps can be dominant businesses and still score lower because the definition of alpha here favors “early before the crowd.”

Bloom Energy evidence: AI data centers using fuel cells for power

Gas turbines excluded. This review does not include GE Vernova, Siemens Energy, Mitsubishi Heavy, Capstone, Rolls-Royce turbine exposure, or other combustion-turbine plays. Bloom’s disclosed technology here is solid oxide fuel-cell onsite electricity generation.

Bloom has direct evidence of AI/data-center use, including AEP’s signed supply agreement and 100 MW order, Oracle’s onsite fuel-cell deployments and later 1.2 GW contracted / up-to-2.8 GW master services agreement, CoreWeave’s AI cloud data-center deployment, Equinix’s >100 MW data-center collaboration, and Brookfield’s up-to-$5B AI infrastructure partnership.

DateCounterpartyEvidence of AI data-center power useContract / order proofSource note
2024-07-16CoreWeave / Chirisa Technology Parksstrategic partnership; Bloom will deploy proprietary solid-oxide fuel cells to generate onsite power at a Volo, Illinois high-performance data center for cloud-based AI computing models; commissioning targeted Q3 2025signed partnership / deployment announcementBloom Energy newsroom, lines 150-154
2024-11-14American Electric Power (AEP)signed supply agreement for up to 1 GW; AEP placed an order for 100 MW; stated use is to power AI data centers and meet data center customers’ sharply increasing AI loadssigned supply agreement + initial 100 MW orderBloom investor release, lines 40-49
2025-02-20Equinixexpanded data-center power agreement exceeding 100 MW; 75 MW operational and 30 MW under construction across 19 IBX data centers in six states; increasing energy needs of AI-driven computing citedexpanded 10-year collaboration / deployed + under-construction capacityBloom investor release, lines 40-49
2025-07-24Oracle Cloud InfrastructureBloom will deploy fuel cells at select OCI U.S. data centers to support AI/cloud workloads and deliver onsite power within 90 days; Bloom states it has deployed over 400 MW to power data centers worldwidedeployment agreement / collaborationBloom investor release, lines 40-54
2025-10-13Brookfield$5B strategic AI infrastructure partnership; Bloom to become preferred onsite power provider for Brookfield global AI factories; Brookfield to invest up to $5B to deploy Bloom fuel-cell technologystrategic partnership / investment commitment up to $5BBloom investor release, lines 40-54
2026-04-13Oracle Cloud Infrastructureexpanded master services agreement; initial 1.2 GW contracted and deploying; MSA supports up to 2.8 GW of Bloom fuel-cell capacity for Oracle AI/cloud infrastructure; warrant issued to Oraclemaster services agreement + contracted 1.2 GW + warrantBloom investor release, lines 40-58

Bloom stock-price context

Latest fetched BE data in this session: price $290.52, market cap $92.9B, trailing EPS -7,263.0. Bloom’s FY2026 guidance is revenue $3.4B–$3.8B and non-GAAP EPS $1.85–$2.25; at $290.52, that implies roughly 129x–157x guided non-GAAP EPS. The business evidence is strong, but the stock no longer screens as “unknown.”

100INDIindie SemiconductorUSUnited StatesNASDAQanalog / power / RF41$4.39$875.0M-5.8selective AI/edge/power exposure; less direct than HBM, advanced packaging and AI networking names.TSM, ASX, AMKR, 005930.KS, UMC

Relationship network and cash-flow map

The direction of the edge is generally buyer/user → supplier/provider, so cash flows usually move in the same direction. Example: a fabless AI chip vendor pays a foundry for wafers, pays an OSAT/packaging partner for assembly/test, and its bill of materials pulls cash toward HBM memory suppliers. Fabs and memory makers then pay lithography, etch/deposition, inspection, test, materials, wafer and subsystem vendors.

Company-by-company connection cards

Evidence book

#SourceLinkWhat it supports
1Bloom / Oracle 2026 MSAsourceInitial 1.2 GW contracted, MSA up to 2.8 GW, AI/cloud infrastructure use, warrant to Oracle.
2Bloom / AEP 2024sourceSupply agreement up to 1 GW and 100 MW initial order for AI data centers.
3Bloom / Oracle 2025sourceOCI fuel-cell deployment for AI/cloud data centers in 90 days; >400 MW data-center deployment to date.
4Bloom / CoreWeave 2024sourceOnsite Bloom fuel cells for CoreWeave AI data center at Volo, Illinois.
5Bloom / Equinix 2025sourceEquinix collaboration exceeds 100 MW across 19 data centers; AI-driven computing demand cited.
6Bloom / Brookfield 2025source$5B AI infrastructure partnership; preferred onsite power provider for AI factories.
7Bloom Q1 2026 resultssourceQ1 revenue $751.1M, 130.4% YoY; operating cash flow $73.6M; FY2026 guide $3.4B-$3.8B revenue and $1.85-$2.25 non-GAAP EPS.
8NVIDIA FY2026 10-KsourceNVIDIA fabless strategy; uses TSMC and Samsung foundries; buys memory from SK hynix, Micron and Samsung; uses CoWoS and subcontractors.
9AMD FY2025 10-KsourceAMD uses TSMC, GF, UMC and Samsung for wafers; ATMP JVs with Tongfu; SPIL/KYEC services; OpenAI 6 GW GPU purchase agreement.
10Broadcom FY2025 10-KsourceAI semiconductor solutions include custom accelerators/XPUs, Ethernet silicon, NICs, PHYs, optical components, racks and systems.
11Marvell FY2026 10-KsourceData center $6.1B / 74% of revenue; custom ASIC, silicon photonics, CPO, AEC DSPs and AI interconnect.
12TSMC 2025 annual reportsourceRobust AI-related demand; Foundry 2.0; advanced nodes and advanced packaging / CoWoS with HBM.
13ASML 2025 annual reportsource2025 net sales €32.7B; growth driven by AI; logic foundry growth and HBM/DDR5 memory investment.
14Micron FY2025 10-KsourceCMBU focused on hyperscale cloud and HBM; CMBU revenue $13.52B; AI-driven demand outpacing supply.
15SK hynix FY2025 resultssourceRecord FY2025; HBM revenue more than doubled year-on-year.
16VanEck SMH holdingssourceSemiconductor ETF holdings show crowd ownership / discovery: NVDA 17.01%, TSM 10.50%, AVGO 7.95%, etc. as of 04/30/2026.
17iShares SOXX overviewsourceETF objective and semiconductor value-chain exposure.
Generated research artifact. CSV sidecars are saved as alpha_rankings.csv and semiconductor_relationship_edges.csv. Data is current to the session fetch where live quotes were available; local-exchange non-U.S. quote fields are intentionally blank when not fetched by the finance tool.
